   =20
      When are tokens securities?
   =20
 =20

We have  talked a few  times recently about the US Securities and Exchange =
Commission=E2=80=99s lawsuit against Ripple Labs Inc. Ripple is a company t=
hat is building a blockchain ecosystem; it created and sold a bunch of cryp=
to tokens called XRP to fund that development. The SEC argued that this was=
 a securities offering =E2=80=94=C2=A0Ripple sold quasi-stock in its projec=
t, for money, to build that project =E2=80=94=C2=A0while Ripple argued that=
 it wasn=E2=80=99t. Last month, a federal judge in New York issued a split =
decision:
=09XRP=C2=A0itself is not a security: It is just a token, =E2=80=9Clittle m=
ore than an=C2=A0alphanumeric cryptographic sequence.=E2=80=9D=09When Rippl=
e=C2=A0sold=C2=A0XRP to institutional investors, directly, in negotiated tr=
ansactions, that=C2=A0was=C2=A0a securities offering, because the instituti=
onal investors understood that they were investing money in Ripple=E2=80=99=
s project and hoped to profit from its efforts to increase the value of XRP=
.=09When Ripple sold XRP to retail investors, in anonymous transactions on =
crypto exchanges, that was=C2=A0not=C2=A0a securities offering, because the=
 retail investors didn=E2=80=99t know that they were buying directly from R=
ipple and might not have even read Ripple=E2=80=99s various public advertis=
ements about how they would make the price of XRP go up.

I found this decision pretty incoherent, for reasons we have discussed at  =
length and which I won=E2=80=99t repeat here.=C2=A0Earlier this week, anoth=
er federal judge in New York  declined to follow it:

A federal judge in New York split with another judge who earlier this month=
 ruled that a Ripple Labs token was not a security when sold to the public =
on secondary markets, adding to uncertainty over cryptocurrency regulation.
US District Judge Jed Rakoff on Monday allowed the Securities and Exchange =
Commission to go forward with its case against Terraform Labs Pte and found=
er Do Kwon. In doing so, Rakoff said he rejected the distinction made in th=
e Ripple case between public and institutional sales. =E2=80=A6
=E2=80=9CThe court declines to draw a distinction between these coins based=
 on their manner of sale, such that coins sold directly to institutional in=
vestors are considered securities and those sold through secondary market t=
ransactions to retail investors are not,=E2=80=9D Rakoff said in the Terra =
decision. =E2=80=9CIn doing so, the court rejects the approach recently ado=
pted by another judge of this district in a similar case.=E2=80=9D

Here is Judge Rakoff=E2=80=99s opinion. He goes on (citations omitted):

[In Ripple], that court found that,=C2=A0=E2=80=9C[w]hereas ... [i]nstituti=
onal [b]uyers reasonably expected that [the defendant crypto-asset company]=
 would use the capital it received from its sales to improve the [crypto-as=
set] ecosystem and thereby increase the price of [the crypto-asset],=E2=80=
=9D those who purchased their coins through secondary transactions had no r=
easonable basis to expect the same. According to that court, this was becau=
se the re-sale purchasers could not have known if their payments went to th=
e defendant, as opposed to the third-party entity who sold them the coin. W=
hatever expectation of profit they had could not, according to that court, =
be ascribed to defendants=E2=80=99 efforts.
But Howey makes no such distinction between purchasers. And it makes good s=
ense that it did not. That a purchaser bought the coins directly from the d=
efendants or, instead, in a secondary resale transaction has no impact on w=
hether a reasonable individual would objectively view the defendants=E2=80=
=99 actions and statements as evincing a promise of profits based on their =
efforts. Indeed, if the Amended Complaint=E2=80=99s allegations are taken a=
s true -- as, again, they must be at this stage -- the defendants=E2=80=99 =
embarked on a public campaign to encourage both retail and institutional in=
vestors to buy their crypto-assets by touting the profitability of the cryp=
toassets and the managerial and technical skills that would allow the defen=
dants to maximize returns on the investors=E2=80=99 coins.

Yeah, look, this is correct. As I  wrote about Ripple, it would be very wei=
rd if the securities laws allowed companies to sell investment opportunitie=
s to the general public by advertising widely that they were good investmen=
t opportunities, as long as no one could prove that any individual buyer ha=
d seen those advertisements. The question is =E2=80=9Cwhether a reasonable =
individual would objectively view the defendants=E2=80=99 actions and state=
ments as evincing a promise of profits based on their efforts.=E2=80=9D
Still, one important point about Judge Rakoff=E2=80=99s opinion is that he =
agrees with Ripple, Terraform, most of the crypto industry and Judge Analis=
a Torres (the Ripple judge), and disagrees with the SEC, about whether toke=
ns are=C2=A0themselves=C2=A0securities:
XRP, as a digital token, is not in and of itself a =E2=80=9Ccontract, trans=
action[,] or scheme=E2=80=9D that embodies the Howey requirements of an inv=
estment contract.=C2=A0Rather, the Court examines the totality of circumsta=
nces surrounding Defendants=E2=80=99 different transactions and schemes inv=
olving the sale and distribution of XRP.=C2=A0
He just concludes that all of those sales were securities offerings.=C2=A0
I don=E2=80=99t know what to make of that. Judge Rakoff was deciding a moti=
on to dismiss in a particular case, not trying to make broad crypto law for=
 every case. But what if this is the law?
=09Crypto tokens are not securities.=09Crypto issuers who sell those tokens=
 to fund their projects are doing securities offerings.

What would that mean for=C2=A0crypto exchanges? The  action right now in SE=
C crypto enforcement is largely against exchanges, arguing that they are ru=
nning illegal securities exchanges and should stop. But if the tokens thems=
elves are not securities, does that mean that the exchanges are entirely of=
f the hook? Or, if the issuances of =E2=80=9Ctoken plus totality of circums=
tances=E2=80=9D are securities offerings, does that mean that when the toke=
ns start trading they are trading along with the totality of their circumst=
ances, and are thus securities? If you buy an XRP or a Luna (Terra=E2=80=99=
s token), are you just buying a digital token, or are you expecting =E2=80=
=9Cthe profitability of the cryptoassets=E2=80=9D from=C2=A0=E2=80=9Cthe ma=
nagerial and technical skills that would allow the [issuers] to maximize re=

This particular opportunity stemmed from a type of subsidy agreement called=
 =E2=80=9Ccontracts for difference,=E2=80=9D or =E2=80=9CCfDs=E2=80=9D for =
short. The CfD for Drax=E2=80=99s Unit 1 follows a straightforward logic.

Then they figure out how much the firm can sell its electricity for on the =

=E2=80=94 the =E2=80=9Cdifference=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 via charges added to t=

We  talked yesterday about Fitch Ratings=E2=80=99=C2=A0decision to downgrad=

=E2=80=99s (which cut the US to AA+ in 2011) and breaking from Moody=E2=80=
=99s (which still has it at Aaa). My main reaction was, meh, whatever. Fitc=
h=E2=80=99s downgrade does not add a lot of=C2=A0information=C2=A0to the ma=
rket; perhaps someone bought Treasuries thinking they were a safe investmen=
t but changed her mind after seeing Fitch=E2=80=99s downgrade, but that wou=
ld be pretty weird.
And while there are some contexts in which investors are=C2=A0forced=C2=A0t=
o sell bonds when they are downgraded =E2=80=94=C2=A0if you are an investme=
nt-grade manager and a bond goes from BBB- to BB+, it is no longer investme=
nt-grade and you might have to get rid of it
  [3]
=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0it seems unlikely that there are any investors like th=
at=C2=A0in Treasuries. =E2=80=9CBecause Treasury securities are such an imp=
ortant asset class, most investment mandates and regulatory regimes refer t=

man Sachs Group Inc. research note pointed out, so nobody should care much =

Still one reader pointed out to me that the interesting consequences are fo=
r US non-government debt, or rather not-quite-government debt. If you are a=
n AAA-rated US company, this downgrade is fine: Fitch left the US=E2=80=99s=
 =E2=80=9Ccountry ceiling=E2=80=9D at AAA, meaning that AAA-rated corporate=
s are not affected by the downgrade.

=C2=A0Bloomberg=E2=80=99s=C2=A0Josyana Joshua=C2=A0and Sonali Basak  note:

S, at least for two companies =E2=80=94 Microsoft Corp. and Johnson & Johns=
on.
Citigroup Inc.=E2=80=99s head of global debt capital markets, Richard Zoghe=
b, said the downgrade may actually benefit the small group of companies tha=
t have a credit rating as high or higher than the US. Investors could begin=
 replacing sovereign bonds in their portfolios with the highly rated compan=
ies, as some did a few years ago during the European sovereign crisis, he s=

=E2=80=9CThe bad news is it is a very small group of companies that have ra=

=9D Zogheb said on Bloomberg Television Wednesday. =E2=80=9CThere is only s=

We have talked from   time to   time around here about a corporate financin=
g model that I first heard from  Joe Weisenthal: If you have found a way to=
 make some product more cheaply than a big publicly traded incumbent manufa=
cturer,
  [5]
 you could monetize that not by selling the product at a profit, but by=C2=
=A0shorting the incumbent=E2=80=99s stock=C2=A0and giving the product away =
for free. You lose money on every product, but you take away a lot of marke=
t share from the incumbent, whose stock will go down, giving you enough pro=
fit to more than cover your expenses.
We talk about this idea because it exists as an amusing theoretical possibi=
lity =E2=80=94=C2=A0=E2=80=9Ca strange and ghostly form of capitalism,=E2=
=80=9D   I once called it, where =E2=80=9Cyou make money through the abstra=
ct workings of the financial system=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94=C2=A0but there are a=
 ton of practical problems and I cannot think of any examples of anyone rea=
lly doing it.
  [6]
=C2=A0It combines all the problems of short selling =E2=80=94=C2=A0it=E2=80=
=99s risky, your thesis might be right but the stock might surge for other =
reasons, it=E2=80=99s unpopular, you=E2=80=99ll get a lot of regulatory scr=
utiny, etc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0with all the problems of building a real busines=
s, and adds some extra problems from the combination. (If you are shorting =
a stock based on your own business=E2=80=99s information, is that illegal =
=E2=80=9Cshadow trading=E2=80=9D?
  [7]
)
But at the American Prospect today, Maureen Tkacik reports on  someone who =
actually tried it?=C2=A0

About a decade ago, a hedge fund manager (who asked to remain nameless) got=
 a call from a young short seller about a pharma company whose stock price =
they were both betting would fall. The company, Questcor, had been a 50-cen=
t stock just a few years earlier, but a new CEO had raised the price of its=
 flagship product by 1,300 percent, and now the stock was approaching 50 do=
llars. =E2=80=A6
The flagship drug, HP Acthar, positively reeked. =E2=80=A6 And yet prescrip=
tions for the $28,000 vials kept rolling in, the stock kept going up, and a=
ll the bearish bets were now bleeding red ink.
But the kid had a plan. He=E2=80=99d recently founded a biotech company of =
his own, which was in the final stage of negotiations to acquire the domest=
ic distribution rights to a European drug that was almost a precise synthet=
ic equivalent to Acthar, minus a few amino acids=E2=80=94so it wouldn=E2=80=
=99t be covered by any exclusivity deal. EU data suggested the drug, Synact=
hen, would work exactly the same as Acthar, but its owner had never applied=
 for FDA approval. The neurological disorder was rare enough that clinical =
trials would be fast and cheap, thanks to federal policies that strongly pr=
ivilege so-called =E2=80=9Corphan drugs=E2=80=9D and make them exponentiall=
y cheaper to bring to market than drugs affecting a wider population. ...
Acthar was so crazily priced, he could undercut it by a factor of ten and s=
till profit handsomely. But he=E2=80=99d do more damage to Questcor=E2=80=
=94and make more money on the short=E2=80=94if he gave it away for free.
It sounded like a pharma version of The Big Short, only instead of getting =
rich speaking truth about a tidal wave of foreclosures that was about to wi=
pe out the global banking system, they=E2=80=99d get rich saving babies=E2=
=80=99 lives while immiserating a bunch of sketchy corporate executives. It=
 was a perfect plan, with one little flaw: There were these things called s=
ecurities laws. =E2=80=9CSTOP TALKING NOW,=E2=80=9D the hedge fund manager =
interrupted. =E2=80=9CYou can=E2=80=99t be telling me any of this!=E2=80=9D

In the event this plan was ruined not by securities laws but by Questcor bu=
ying the competitor drug out from under him:
A few months later, the day before the kid was scheduled to wire over his $=

=99s] short position in Questcor, coupled with a host of other shorts in su=
spect drug companies that refused to shrivel promptly, was likely killing h=

Yeah just seems like a risky way to make a living. Anyway the kid in this a=

That=E2=80=99s a report from Reed Albergotti at Semafor, and there is a=C2=
=A0lot going on there!=C2=A0For one thing, the US  Securities and Exchange =

ght cases against Binance, and have said pretty rude things about it;=C2=A0=

=99t really seem like they did.

=9Cehh let=E2=80=99s just indict them=E2=80=9D and it  turned out fine.=C2=

omers are=C2=A0more=C2=A0worried about legal compliance than bank customers=

 the SEC and CFTC cases against Binance is that they are=C2=A0primarily=C2=
=A0about technical failures to comply with US securities and commodities ru=

tering it with the CFTC,=E2=80=9D =E2=80=9Cyou listed security tokens on yo=
ur exchange without registering it with the SEC,=E2=80=9D that sort of thin=

rd customer money, but they are not the main thrust of those cases, and the=

 Inc. =E2=80=94=C2=A0is not really vulnerable to a bank run; if everyone as=

Broadly speaking, a lot of financial-services businesses involve some mix o=
f (1)=C2=A0sourcing=C2=A0deals, (2)=C2=A0evaluating=C2=A0deals=C2=A0and (3)=
 executing deals. In Step 1, you build up a network of potential clients (c=
ounterparties investors, targets, whatever), cold-call them, meet with them=
, build a public profile so they call you, whatever. In Step 2, you look at=
 the deals that are available to you, do the ones that you think will make =
you money and avoid the ones that will lose you money. In Step 3=C2=A0 you =
structure the investment and negotiate the contract and get the funding and=
 the regulatory approvals and so forth.
If you are a senior person at any sort of financial-services business you a=
re probably good at all three of these elements of the job.
  [9]
 If you are on your first day out of college at any sort of financial-servi=
ces business, you are probably bad at all of them, but you have to start so=
mewhere. Where do you start? If you are a very junior=C2=A0investment banke=
r, you start mostly by learning evaluation skills (you build financial mode=
ls to see which deals are good) and execution skills (you populate the data=
 room). (This is also roughly how you start out in private equity.
  [10]
) You make pitchbooks to try to win business, sure, but that is support wor=
k; you are not going out alone to pitch clients on deals in your first week=
. The classic Wall Street career path begins with financial=C2=A0analysis; =
once you are good enough at analysis they will let you out of the building =
to impress clients with your charm and, also, your analytical abilities. Ev=
entually you build up enough credibility that you can spend most of your ti=
me flying around meeting with potential clients to pitch them on your talen=
ts, but at the beginning you are mostly modeling and executing deals that m=
ore senior people bring in.
  [11]

It is interesting that in venture capital the path is somewhat reversed, an=
d you start out as a VC intern by cold-calling to find potential deals? Lik=
e you walk in and they hand you a phone and you dial up tech companies and =
say =E2=80=9Chello would you be interested in a venture capital investment=
=E2=80=9D until someone says yes? What if they say yes? You can=E2=80=99t j=
ust give them money! You don=E2=80=99t know what you=E2=80=99re doing! Some=
one more senior has to figure out if the company is good, how much to pay f=
or it=C2=A0and how to structure the deal. You are just there to network.
I suppose that part of the reason for this is that estimating the value of =
an investment in a public company is reasonably=C2=A0susceptible to standar=
dized methods =E2=80=94=C2=A0there are textbooks, you can learn how to buil=
d a discounted cash flow or leveraged buyout model and then practice doing =
it a lot =E2=80=94=C2=A0while estimating the value of a pre-revenue tech st=
artup requires more guesswork and gut feel. Also I suppose the founders of =
tech startups are in their 20s while the VC partners are in their 50s, so t=
he 20-something VC interns might be better suited to network with the found=
ers.

ing in a cost of about $181 per ton for much of its fuel. In April 2022, th=
e company could sell such pellets for an almost 70% markup, according to da=
ta provided by the market research firm Argus Media Ltd. Profits from those=
 sales would not be capped.=E2=80=9D Also: =E2=80=9CThe government took a l=
imited approach to using CfDs for biomass; it approved only one of Drax=E2=
=80=99s available generators, Unit 1, for a deal to begin in December 2016.=
 Drax also converted three other units, which continued collecting subsidie=
s from the earlier initiative, which is limited to about =C2=A3650 million =
per year. Last year, Drax increased output at those three generators, which=
 aren=E2=80=99t required to send money to consumers.=E2=80=9D Basically the=
y can shift production between the capped-and-floored CfD generator (subsid=
ized when prices are low) and the uncapped non-CfD generators (lucrative wh=
en prices are high).


  [3] Most actual mandates are more nuanced than this and don=E2=80=99t for=
ce you to dump stuff instantly, but you get the idea.


  [4] The intuition here is that if a country=E2=80=99s sovereign debt is r=
ated BB, there is enough shakiness in its economy and institutions that non=
e of its companies can really be AAA, so a country ceiling will tend to tra=
ck the sovereign rating. But it doesn=E2=80=99t have to match exactly.


  [5] I am not sure that this is strictly necessary; in the right condition=
s I suppose you could make the product as expensively as the incumbent, or =
more so, but just underprice it. Ideally you would be building a sustainabl=
e long-term business and use the short sales as sort of early-ish-stage cap=
ital, but I guess you could go in other directions.


  [6] There is  this story of a funeral-services-price-comparison company s=
horting a big funeral-services firm in small size, but that=E2=80=99s not r=
eally the same thing.


  [7] I think in the abstract the answer is no: If this is your business mo=
del, you are not violating any obligations to anyone. But in the real world=
 there might be complications around misappropriating, like, customer data =
to inform your short.


  [8] Particularly not if the biggest loser is your affiliated trading firm=
, as was the case at FTX.


  [9] If you are bad at one it is execution, which is probably the easiest =
to specialize and outsource.


  [10] In the sense that the first-day-out-of-college job for most private =
equity professionals is being an investment banking analyst. Still even whe=
n you do start in PE you are probably doing a fair amount of modeling and e=
xecution.


  [11] Sometimes banks partially reverse this by   hiring the young adult c=
hildren of important clients as junior bankers, not because they are good a=
t financial modeling but because they are (presumably) good at bringing in =
their parents=E2=80=99 business. But people get mad about this; it=E2=80=99=
s not how it=E2=80=99s supposed to work.

10px;">
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;"><strong>Programming note:</strong><em> Money S=
tuff will be off tomorrow, back on Monday.</em></p>
<table class=3D"header" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" cellp=
adding=3D"0">
<tr>
<td class=3D"body-header-container" style=3D"border-collapse: collapse; mso=

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=
, Helvetica, sans-serif; letter-spacing: -1px; font-size: 20px; border-bott=
om-width: 1px; border-bottom-style: dotted; margin: 0px; padding: 16px 0px =
5px;">When are tokens securities?</h2>
</td>
</tr>
</table>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">We have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/32287636.260410/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGlu=
aW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDctMTQvcmlwcGxlLWlzLWEtc2VjdXJpdHktYW5kLWl0LWlzbi1=
0P2NtcGlkPUJCRDA4MDMyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT=
1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMDgwMyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07=
ceef4b8524a319a23cBb268d2cc" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope"=
 target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !importan=
t; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-st=
yle: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> talked</a> a few <a href=3D"https://l=
ink.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32287636.260410/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb2=
1iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDctMTcvYnJhbmNoZXMtbWFrZS1iYW5rL=
XJ1bnMtaGFyZGVyP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA4MDMyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwm=
dXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMDgwMyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXl=
zdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB10042842" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> times</a> recently abou=
t the US Securities and Exchange Commission=E2=80=99s lawsuit against Rippl=
e Labs Inc. Ripple is a company that is building a blockchain ecosystem; it=
 created and sold a bunch of crypto tokens called XRP to fund that developm=
ent. The SEC argued that this was a securities offering =E2=80=94=C2=A0Ripp=
le sold quasi-stock in its project, for money, to build that project =E2=80=
=94=C2=A0while Ripple argued that it wasn=E2=80=99t. Last month, a federal =
judge in New York issued <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com=
/click/32287636.260410/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3NldHMuYndieC5pby9kb2N1bWVudHMvdXNlcnMv=
aXFqV0hCRmRmeElVL3JWcXlMRnlFWm56OC92MA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB956690e0" i=
temprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"co=
lor: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; b=
order-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: =
#ccc;">a split decision</a>:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">XRP=C2=A0<em>itself </em>is not a sec=
urity: It is just a token, =E2=80=9Clittle more than an=C2=A0alphanumeric c=
ryptographic sequence.=E2=80=9D</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Wh=
en Ripple=C2=A0<em>sold=C2=A0</em>XRP to institutional investors, directly,=
 in negotiated transactions, that=C2=A0<em>was=C2=A0</em>a securities offer=
ing, because the institutional investors understood that they were investin=
g money in Ripple=E2=80=99s project and hoped to profit from its efforts to=
 increase the value of XRP.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">When R=
ipple sold XRP to retail investors, in anonymous transactions on crypto exc=
hanges, that was=C2=A0<em>not=C2=A0</em>a securities offering, because the =
retail investors didn=E2=80=99t know that they were buying directly from Ri=
pple and might not have even read Ripple=E2=80=99s various public advertise=
ments about how they would make the price of XRP go up.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I found this decision pretty incoherent, for r=
easons we have <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/322=
87636.260410/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMj=
MtMDctMTQvcmlwcGxlLWlzLWEtc2VjdXJpdHktYW5kLWl0LWlzbi10P2NtcGlkPUJCRDA4MDMyM=
19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90=
ZXJtPTIzMDgwMyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cCb26=
8d2cc" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" sty=
le=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width=
: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background=
-color: #ccc;">discussed</a> at <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusin=
ess.com/click/32287636.260410/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL=
2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDctMTcvYnJhbmNoZXMtbWFrZS1iYW5rLXJ1bnMtaGFyZGVyP2NtcGlk=
PUJCRDA4MDMyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV=
0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMDgwMyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b852=
4a319a23cC10042842" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=
=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bord=
er-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: so=
lid; background-color: #ccc;"> length</a> and which I won=E2=80=99t repeat =
here.=C2=A0Earlier this week, another federal judge in New York <a href=3D"=
https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32287636.260410/aHR0cHM6Ly93d=
3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDctMzEvcmlwcGxlLXJ1bGluZy1v=
bi1jcnlwdG8tcmVqZWN0ZWQtYnktZmVkZXJhbC1qdWRnZS1pbi10ZXJyYS1jYXNlP2NtcGlkPUJ=
CRDA4MDMyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dG=
VyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMDgwMyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ249bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a3=
19a23cBf3e42805" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_=
blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bo=
ttom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; =
background-color: #ccc;"> declined to follow it</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A federal judge in New York split with another=
 judge who earlier this month ruled that a Ripple Labs token was not a secu=
rity when sold to the public on secondary markets, adding to uncertainty ov=
er cryptocurrency regulation.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">US District Judge Jed Rakoff on Monday allowed=
 the Securities and Exchange Commission to go forward with its case against=
 Terraform Labs Pte and founder Do Kwon. In doing so, Rakoff said he reject=
ed the distinction made in the Ripple case between public and institutional=
 sales. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CThe court declines to draw a distinct=
ion between these coins based on their manner of sale, such that coins sold=
 directly to institutional investors are considered securities and those so=
ld through secondary market transactions to retail investors are not,=E2=80=
=9D Rakoff said in the Terra decision. =E2=80=9CIn doing so, the court reje=
cts the approach recently adopted by another judge of this district in a si=
milar case.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloomberg=
business.com/click/32287636.260410/aHR0cHM6Ly9hc3NldHMuYndieC5pby9kb2N1bWVu=
dHMvdXNlcnMvaXFqV0hCRmRmeElVL3JfaWtyRXphckhPUS92MA/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23c=
B2a711b07" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank"=
 style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-w=
idth: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; backgr=
ound-color: #ccc;">Judge Rakoff=E2=80=99s opinion</a>. He goes on (citation=
s omitted):</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[In Ripple], that court found that,=C2=A0=E2=
=80=9C[w]hereas ... [i]nstitutional [b]uyers reasonably expected that [the =
defendant crypto-asset company] would use the capital it received from its =
sales to improve the [crypto-asset] ecosystem and thereby increase the pric=
e of [the crypto-asset],=E2=80=9D those who purchased their coins through s=
econdary transactions had no reasonable basis to expect the same. According=
 to that court, this was because the re-sale purchasers could not have know=
n if their payments went to the defendant, as opposed to the third-party en=
tity who sold them the coin. Whatever expectation of profit they had could =
not, according to that court, be ascribed to defendants=E2=80=99 efforts.</=
p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But <em>Howey </em>makes no such distinction b=
etween purchasers. And it makes good sense that it did not. That a purchase=
r bought the coins directly from the defendants or, instead, in a secondary=
 resale transaction has no impact on whether a reasonable individual would =
objectively view the defendants=E2=80=99 actions and statements as evincing=
 a promise of profits based on their efforts. Indeed, if the Amended Compla=
int=E2=80=99s allegations are taken as true -- as, again, they must be at t=
his stage -- the defendants=E2=80=99 embarked on a public campaign to encou=
rage both retail and institutional investors to buy their crypto-assets by =
touting the profitability of the cryptoassets and the managerial and techni=
cal skills that would allow the defendants to maximize returns on the inves=
tors=E2=80=99 coins.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yeah, look, this is correct. As I <a href=3D"h=
ttps://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32287636.260410/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3=
cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDctMTcvYnJhbmNoZXMtbWFrZ=
S1iYW5rLXJ1bnMtaGFyZGVyP2NtcGlkPUJCRDA4MDMyM19NT05FWVNUVUZGJnV0bV9tZWRpdW09=
ZW1haWwmdXRtX3NvdXJjZT1uZXdzbGV0dGVyJnV0bV90ZXJtPTIzMDgwMyZ1dG1fY2FtcGFpZ24=
9bW9uZXlzdHVmZg/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cD10042842" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" i=

 sell investment opportunities to the general public by advertising widely =
that they were good investment opportunities, as long as no one could prove=

=E2=80=9Cwhether a reasonable individual would objectively view the defenda=
nts=E2=80=99 actions and statements as evincing a promise of profits based =
on their efforts.=E2=80=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still, one important point about Judge Rakoff=
=E2=80=99s opinion is that he agrees with Ripple, Terraform, most of the cr=
ypto industry and Judge Analisa Torres (the Ripple judge), and disagrees wi=
th the SEC, about whether tokens are=C2=A0<em>themselves=C2=A0</em>securiti=
es:</p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">XRP, as a digital token, is not in=
 and of itself a =E2=80=9Ccontract, transaction[,] or scheme=E2=80=9D that =
embodies the Howey requirements of an investment contract.=C2=A0Rather, the=
 Court examines the totality of circumstances surrounding Defendants=E2=80=
=99 different transactions and schemes involving the sale and distribution =
of XRP.=C2=A0</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">He just concludes that all of those sales were=
 securities offerings.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I don=E2=80=99t know what to make of that. Jud=
ge Rakoff was deciding a motion to dismiss in a particular case, not trying=
 to make broad crypto law for every case. But what if this is the law?</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Crypto tokens are not securities.</li=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">Crypto issuers who sell those tokens =
to fund their projects are doing securities offerings.</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">What would that mean for=C2=A0<em>crypto excha=
nges</em>? The <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/322=
87636.260410/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9vcGluaW9uL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMj=

2" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=
=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: =
1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-c=
olor: #ccc;"> action right now</a> in SEC crypto enforcement is largely aga=
inst exchanges, arguing that they are running illegal securities exchanges =
and should stop. But if the tokens themselves are not securities, does that=
 mean that the exchanges are entirely off the hook? Or, if the issuances of=
 =E2=80=9Ctoken plus totality of circumstances=E2=80=9D are securities offe=
rings, does that mean that when the tokens start trading they are trading a=

you buy an XRP or a Luna (Terra=E2=80=99s token), are you just buying a dig=

ould allow the [issuers] to maximize returns on the investors=E2=80=99 coin=
s=E2=80=9D?=C2=A0</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here=E2=80=99s a trade:</p>
<ul style=3D"list-style-type: disc; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;">=
=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">I am a widget producer, you are a widg=
et user, and widgets trade in a liquid daily=C2=A0market with lots of buyer=
s and sellers and pretty volatile prices.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom=
: 5px;">We sign a contract saying that I can sell you anywhere between 0 an=
d 100 widgets each day for a year, at my option:=C2=A0Each day, I pick a nu=
mber between 0 and 100, and that=E2=80=99s how many widgets you buy from me=
 that day.</li>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The price you pay is $2=
0 per widget, regardless of the market price that day.</li>
</ul>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Questions:</p>
<ol style=3D"list-style-type: decimal; padding-left: 40px; margin: 16px 0;"=
>=09<li style=3D"margin-bottom: 5px;">The market price of widgets on Monday=
 is $15. How many widgets do I sell you on Monday?</li>=09<li style=3D"marg=
in-bottom: 5px;">The market price of widgets on Wednesday is $25. How many =
widgets do I sell you on Wednesday?</li>
</ol>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I suppose you can imagine some real-world situ=
ations in which the answers are not =E2=80=9C100=E2=80=9D and =E2=80=9Czero=
,=E2=80=9D respectively, but, come on, the answers are =E2=80=9C100=E2=80=
=9D and =E2=80=9Czero,=E2=80=9D respectively. And if you entered into this =
contract, and Wednesday comes along and you are like =E2=80=9Cah, sweet, no=
w to pay $20 for my discounted widgets,=E2=80=9D you will be disappointed.<=
/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Bloomberg=E2=80=99s=C2=A0Todd Gillespie, Gavin=
 Finch, Will Mathis and Jason Grotto have a story about <a href=3D"https://=
link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32287636.260410/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb=
21iZXJnLmNvbS9ncmFwaGljcy8yMDIzLXVrLXBvd2VyLXN0YXRpb25zLWF2b2lkZWQtcGF5aW5n=
LWJhY2stY29uc3VtZXJzLz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwODAzMjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWV=
tYWlsJnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA4MDMmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW=
1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB7bf3816d" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" ite=
mscope=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decora=
tion: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #0000=
00; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> UK electric produ=
cer Drax Group Plc=E2=80=99s green energy subsidies</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Since December 2016, a generator at the firm=
=E2=80=99s giant power plant had received =C2=A31.4 billion ($1.8 billion) =
in green-energy subsidies from consumers =E2=80=94 payments above the marke=
t rate for electricity. The goal of this public aid was to make it profitab=
le for Drax to fuel the unit with =E2=80=9Cbiomass,=E2=80=9D pellets made f=
rom wood that are considered a renewable energy source.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This expensive method for weaning Britain=E2=
=80=99s power grid off fossil fuels contained a consumer safeguard: If elec=
tricity prices ever increased enough that Drax=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9CUnit 1=E2=
=80=9D generator could comfortably make money without subsidies, its earnin=
gs would be capped, and the company would have to send any extra cash back =
to bill payers.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Here is specifically how the subsidy works:</p=
>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">This particular opportunity stemmed from a typ=
e of subsidy agreement called =E2=80=9Ccontracts for difference,=E2=80=9D o=
r =E2=80=9CCfDs=E2=80=9D for short. The CfD for Drax=E2=80=99s Unit 1 follo=
ws a straightforward logic.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">First, negotiators agree on how much it will c=
ost Drax to produce electricity from biomass. They add some extra to ensure=
 a reasonable profit. This total is called the =E2=80=9Cstrike price.=E2=80=
=9D</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Then they figure out how much the firm can sel=
l its electricity for on the wholesale market. If that =E2=80=9Cmarket refe=
rence price=E2=80=9D is lower than the strike price, consumers have to send=
 Drax the rest of the money =E2=80=94 the =E2=80=9Cdifference=E2=80=9D =E2=
=80=94 via charges added to their energy bills.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But if the market reference price rises above =
the strike price, the arrangement flips. The contract holds that Drax, now =
assured of turning a profit, must send the difference back to energy suppli=
ers, who by law then reduce what they charge consumers.</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Ah! If the market price is below $20, they get=
 paid $20.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-1" style=3D"color: =
#000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border=
-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-co=
lor: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[1]</span>
</a>=C2=A0If the market price is above $20, they get paid $20. How much sho=
uld they sell if the market price is below $20? As much as they can. How mu=
ch should they sell if the market price is above $20? Zero<a class=3D"footn=
ote-index" href=3D"#footnote-2" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: n=
one !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bor=
der-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transparent !important=
; display: inline-block;">
<span>[2]</span>
</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">As household energy bills began more than doub=
ling, the company slashed production at Unit 1. Idling the generator for we=
eks at a time =E2=80=94 and instead using others that weren=E2=80=99t requi=
red to cap their earnings =E2=80=94 has let Drax avoid sending consumers an=
 estimated =C2=A3639 million as of July, according to a Bloomberg News anal=
ysis of thousands of power market records.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Meanwhile, the company sold some of its biomas=
s pellets at high prices on the open market, according to its public statem=
ents and to five people with direct knowledge of the biomass market. Drax p=
osted a record =C2=A3731 million in earnings last year, almost double its 2=
021 income.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The company didn=E2=80=99t break any rules. Bu=
t =E2=80=9Cit looks like they are acting in bad faith,=E2=80=9D said Ed Dav=
ey, who was the UK government=E2=80=99s energy secretary when it negotiated=
 the firm=E2=80=99s subsidy deal, and is now leader of the opposition Liber=
al Democrats. =E2=80=9CIt=E2=80=99s against the national interest.=E2=80=9D=
</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I am sorry but you should have done a better j=
ob negotiating the subsidy deal! Put a line in the contract like =E2=80=9Ci=
n any event you=E2=80=99ll always supply at least X megawatt-hours per day =
under this deal.=E2=80=9D Or just have the contract be on a fixed notional =
amount rather than the amount actually supplied. But if you base the contra=
ct on how much electricity they actually supply, and let them decide how mu=
ch to supply, then they will supply a lot when the contract is favorable to=
 them and nothing when it is favorable to you.</p>

ision to downgrade the US sovereign credit rating from AAA to AA+, joining =
Standard & Poor=E2=80=99s (which cut the US to AA+ in 2011) and breakin=
g from Moody=E2=80=99s (which still has it at Aaa). My main reaction was, m=
eh, whatever. Fitch=E2=80=99s downgrade does not add a lot of=C2=A0<em>info=
rmation=C2=A0</em>to the market; perhaps someone bought Treasuries thinking=
 they were a safe investment but changed her mind after seeing Fitch=E2=80=
=99s downgrade, but that would be pretty weird.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">And while there are some contexts in which inv=
estors are=C2=A0<em>forced=C2=A0</em>to sell bonds when they are downgraded=
 =E2=80=94=C2=A0if you are an investment-grade manager and a bond goes from=
 BBB- to BB+, it is no longer investment-grade and you might have to get ri=
d of it<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-3" style=3D"color: #00=
0000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bo=
ttom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color=

<span>[3]</span>
</a>=C2=A0=E2=80=94=C2=A0it seems unlikely that there are any investors lik=
e that=C2=A0<em>in Treasuries</em>. =E2=80=9CBecause Treasury securities ar=
e such an important asset class, most investment mandates and regulatory re=
gimes refer to them specifically, rather than AAA-rated government debt,=E2=
=80=9D a Goldman Sachs Group Inc. research note pointed out, so nobody shou=
ld care much about the downgrade.=C2=A0</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Still one reader pointed out to me that the in=
teresting consequences are for US non-government debt, or rather not-quite-=
government debt. If you are an AAA-rated US <em>company</em>, this downgrad=
e is fine: Fitch left the US=E2=80=99s =E2=80=9Ccountry ceiling=E2=80=9D at=
 AAA, meaning that AAA-rated corporates are not affected by the downgrade.<=
a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-4" style=3D"color: #000000; te=
xt-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-col=
or: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transp=
arent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[4]</span>
</a>=C2=A0Bloomberg=E2=80=99s=C2=A0Josyana Joshua=C2=A0and Sonali Basak <a =
href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32287636.260410/aHR0c=

waWQ9QkJEMDgwMzIzX01PTkVZU1RVRkYmdXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbCZ1dG1fc291cmNlPW5ld3=
NsZXR0ZXImdXRtX3Rlcm09MjMwODAzJnV0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1tb25leXN0dWZm/607f07ceef4b=
8524a319a23cB99adad6b" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"itemscope" targe=
t=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; bor=
der-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: s=
olid; background-color: #ccc;"> note</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">There is likely to be a silver lining in Fitch=
=E2=80=99s downgrade of the US, at least for two companies =E2=80=94 Micros=
oft Corp. and Johnson & Johnson.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Citigroup Inc.=E2=80=99s head of global debt c=
apital markets, Richard Zogheb, said the downgrade may actually benefit the=
 small group of companies that have a credit rating as high or higher than =
the US. Investors could begin replacing sovereign bonds in their portfolios=
 with the highly rated companies, as some did a few years ago during the Eu=
ropean sovereign crisis, he said.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Moreover, the few companies that are rated on-=
par with the US could see their spreads tighten in line with US Treasury bo=
nds.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">=E2=80=9CThe bad news is it is a very small gr=
oup of companies that have ratings at or above the current sovereign rate f=
or the US government,=E2=80=9D Zogheb said on Bloomberg Television Wednesda=
y. =E2=80=9CThere is only so much of your portfolio that you can replace of=
 sovereigns into these multinational, highly-rated areas.=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But that=E2=80=99s not true of every AAA-rated=
 US company. Fitch=C2=A0<em>did=C2=A0</em>downgrade <a href=3D"https://link=
.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32287636.260410/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuZml0Y2hyY=
XRpbmdzLmNvbS9yZXNlYXJjaC9ub24tYmFuay1maW5hbmNpYWwtaW5zdGl0dXRpb25zL2ZpdGNo=
LWRvd25ncmFkZXMtZmFubmllLW1hZS1mcmVkZGllLW1hYy10by1hYS1mb2xsb3dpbmctc292ZXJ=
laWduLWFjdGlvbi1vdXRsb29rLXN0YWJsZS0wMi0wOC0yMDIzIzp-OnRleHQ9Rml0Y2glMjBSYX=
RpbmdzJTIwJTJEJTIwQ2hpY2FnbyUyMCUyRCUyMDAyJTIwQXVnLGFhJTJCJyUyMGZyb20lMjAnY=
WFhJy4/607f07ceef4b8524a319a23cBf0a5bc8b" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=
=3D"itemscope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: =
none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; bo=
rder-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">two big US companies yes=
terday</a>:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Fitch Ratings downgraded the credit scores of =
Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to AA+ from AAA, a day after it cut the US sover=
eign credit rating.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The downgrades of the two government-sponsored=
 enterprises are consistent with its downgrade of US government debt, Fitch=
 said in a press release on Wednesday. The move was =E2=80=9Cnot being driv=
en by fundamental credit, capital or liquidity deterioration at the firms,=
=E2=80=9D it said.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac benefit from implic=
it government support, Fitch said. The two enterprises help to backstop the=
 multi-trillion dollar market for US home mortgages.</p>

cking, backing that used to be AAA-rated and is now only AA+: agency mortga=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I am not sure how much this matters either, bu=

could be posting these securities as collateral in some trade that requires=

ernment-supported asset-backed-securities are, maybe, in some contexts, jus=
t AAA bonds. Or=C2=A0rather they were, and=C2=A0now they are not.</p>

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But at the American Prospect today, Maureen Tk=

-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;"> some=

<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">About a decade ago, a hedge fund manager (who =
asked to remain nameless) got a call from a young short seller about a phar=
ma company whose stock price they were both betting would fall. The company=
, Questcor, had been a 50-cent stock just a few years earlier, but a new CE=
O had raised the price of its flagship product by 1,300 percent, and now th=
e stock was approaching 50 dollars. =E2=80=A6</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The flagship drug, HP Acthar, positively reeke=
d. =E2=80=A6 And yet prescriptions for the $28,000 vials kept rolling in, t=
he stock kept going up, and all the bearish bets were now bleeding red ink.=
</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">But the kid had a plan. He=E2=80=99d recently =
founded a biotech company of his own, which was in the final stage of negot=
iations to acquire the domestic distribution rights to a European drug that=
 was almost a precise synthetic equivalent to Acthar, minus a few amino aci=
ds=E2=80=94so it wouldn=E2=80=99t be covered by any exclusivity deal. EU da=
ta suggested the drug, Synacthen, would work exactly the same as Acthar, bu=
t its owner had never applied for FDA approval. The neurological disorder w=
as rare enough that clinical trials would be fast and cheap, thanks to fede=
ral policies that strongly privilege so-called =E2=80=9Corphan drugs=E2=80=
=9D and make them exponentially cheaper to bring to market than drugs affec=
ting a wider population. ...</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Acthar was so crazily priced, he could undercu=
t it by a factor of ten and still profit handsomely. But he=E2=80=99d do mo=
re damage to Questcor=E2=80=94and make more money on the short=E2=80=94if h=
e gave it away for free.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It sounded like a pharma version of The Big Sh=
ort, only instead of getting rich speaking truth about a tidal wave of fore=
closures that was about to wipe out the global banking system, they=E2=80=
=99d get rich saving babies=E2=80=99 lives while immiserating a bunch of sk=
etchy corporate executives. It was a perfect plan, with one little flaw: Th=
ere were these things called securities laws. =E2=80=9CSTOP TALKING NOW,=E2=
=80=9D the hedge fund manager interrupted. =E2=80=9CYou can=E2=80=99t be te=
lling me any of this!=E2=80=9D</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">In the event this plan was ruined not by secur=
ities laws but by Questcor buying the competitor drug out from under him:</=
p>
<blockquote><p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">A few months later, the day before=
 the kid was scheduled to wire over his $16 million and assume the distribu=
tion rights to Synacthen, Questcor swooped in and bid $135 million, plus a =
handful of potential bonuses the kid figured would more than double the sti=
cker price. =E2=80=A6=C2=A0[The kid=E2=80=99s] short position in Questcor, =
coupled with a host of other shorts in suspect drug companies that refused =
to shrivel promptly, was likely killing him.=C2=A0</p></blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Yeah just seems like a risky way to make a liv=
ing. Anyway the kid in this anecdote was Martin Shkreli, which seems exactl=
y right.</p>

<h2 style=3D"font-weight: bold; font-family: "Arial Black", Arial=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Broadly speaking, a lot of financial-services =
businesses involve some mix of (1)=C2=A0<em>sourcing=C2=A0</em>deals, (2)=
=C2=A0<em>evaluating=C2=A0</em>deals=C2=A0and (3) <em>executing </em>deals.=
 In Step 1, you build up a network of potential clients (counterparties inv=
estors, targets, whatever), cold-call them, meet with them, build a public =
profile so they call you, whatever. In Step 2, you look at the deals that a=
re available to you, do the ones that you think will make you money and avo=
id the ones that will lose you money. In Step 3=C2=A0 you structure the inv=
estment and negotiate the contract and get the funding and the regulatory a=
pprovals and so forth.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">If you are a senior person at any sort of fina=
ncial-services business you are probably good at all three of these element=
s of the job.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-9" style=3D"colo=
r: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; bor=
der-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background=
-color: transparent !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[9]</span>
</a> If you are on your first day out of college at any sort of financial-s=
ervices business, you are probably bad at all of them, but you have to star=
t somewhere. Where do you start? If you are a very junior=C2=A0investment b=
anker, you start mostly by learning evaluation skills (you build financial =
models to see which deals are good) and execution skills (you populate the =
data room). (This is also roughly how you start out in private equity.<a cl=
ass=3D"footnote-index" href=3D"#footnote-10" style=3D"color: #000000; text-=
decoration: none !important; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color:=
 #000000; border-bottom-style: none !important; background-color: transpare=
nt !important; display: inline-block;">
<span>[10]</span>
</a>) You make pitchbooks to try to win business, sure, but that is support=
 work; you are not going out alone to pitch clients on deals in your first =
week. The classic Wall Street career path begins with financial=C2=A0<em>an=
alysis</em>; once you are good enough at analysis they will let you out of =
the building to impress clients with your charm and, also, your analytical =
abilities. Eventually you build up enough credibility that you can spend mo=
st of your time flying around meeting with potential clients to pitch them =
on your talents, but at the beginning you are mostly modeling and executing=
 deals that more senior people bring in.<a class=3D"footnote-index" href=3D=
"#footnote-11" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !important; b=
order-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bottom-style:=
 none !important; background-color: transparent !important; display: inline=
-block;">
<span>[11]</span>
</a></p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">It is interesting that in venture capital the =
path is somewhat reversed, and you start out as a VC intern by cold-calling=
 to find potential deals? Like you walk in and they hand you a phone and yo=
u dial up tech companies and say =E2=80=9Chello would you be interested in =
a venture capital investment=E2=80=9D until someone says yes? What if they =
say yes? You can=E2=80=99t just give them money! You don=E2=80=99t know wha=
t you=E2=80=99re doing! Someone more senior has to figure out if the compan=
y is good, how much to pay for it=C2=A0and how to structure the deal. You a=
re just there to network.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">I suppose that part of the reason for this is =
that estimating the value of an investment in a public company is <em>reaso=
nably=C2=A0</em>susceptible to standardized methods =E2=80=94=C2=A0there ar=
e textbooks, you can learn how to build a discounted cash flow or leveraged=
 buyout model and then practice doing it a lot =E2=80=94=C2=A0while estimat=
ing the value of a pre-revenue tech startup requires more guesswork and gut=
 feel. Also I suppose the founders of tech startups are in their 20s while =
the VC partners are in their 50s, so the 20-something VC interns might be b=
etter suited to network with the founders.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">Anyway Bloomberg=E2=80=99s Magdalena Del Valle=
 has <a href=3D"https://link.mail.bloombergbusiness.com/click/32287636.2604=
10/aHR0cHM6Ly93d3cuYmxvb21iZXJnLmNvbS9uZXdzL2FydGljbGVzLzIwMjMtMDgtMDMvaW50=
ZXJuLWh1bmdlci1nYW1lcy1pbi1mdWxsLXN3aW5nLXdpdGgtdmMtc2hvcC1zLXB1YmxpYy1yYW5=
raW5ncz9jbXBpZD1CQkQwODAzMjNfTU9ORVlTVFVGRiZ1dG1fbWVkaXVtPWVtYWlsJnV0bV9zb3=
VyY2U9bmV3c2xldHRlciZ1dG1fdGVybT0yMzA4MDMmdXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPW1vbmV5c3R1ZmY/6=
07f07ceef4b8524a319a23cB4b05cdcd" itemprop=3D"StoryLink" itemscope=3D"items=
cope" target=3D"_blank" style=3D"color: #000000; text-decoration: none !imp=
ortant; border-bottom-width: 1px; border-bottom-color: #000000; border-bott=
om-style: solid; background-color: #ccc;">  a funny story on VC interns</a>=
:</p>
<blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The summer interns at=C2=A0Insight Partners=C2=
=A0are learning the firm=E2=80=99s=C2=A0number one value=C2=A0=E2=80=94 =E2=
=80=9Chunger to win=E2=80=9D =E2=80=94 the hard way.</p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The New York-based company, known for taking e=
arly stakes in ventures like=C2=A0DocuSign Inc.=C2=A0and=C2=A0Shopify Inc.,=
 publicly ranks its investing interns using metrics like their phone calls,=
 emails, and meetings, according to people with knowledge of the matter. Th=
e rankings are displayed on a so-called leaderboard for the firm, said the =
people, who asked not to be identified because they aren=E2=80=99t authoriz=
ed to speak about it. Some full-time employees are also ranked, they said.<=
/p>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">The ranking isn=E2=80=99t an automatic ticket =
to getting a job. It=E2=80=99s one of many data points taken into considera=
tion, including participation in meetings and attendance at =E2=80=9Clunch =
and learns,=E2=80=9D according to a person familiar with Insight=E2=80=99s =
hiring practices, who said interns are told not to worry about their rankin=
g.=C2=A0</p>
</blockquote>
<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">They get ranked on how many cold calls they ma=
ke! Also I love the idea of giving your interns a public ranking and tellin=
g them not to worry about it, what a great management technique, that=E2=80=
=99s how you keep them on their toes.</p>

<div id=3D"footnote-7" class=3D"footnote-content" style=3D"font-style: ital=

<p style=3D"margin: 16px 0;">[7] I think in the abstract the answer is no: =
